en.extract:
The black-rumped magpie (Pica bottanensis) is a species of magpie native to west-central China and Bhutan, and possibly also irregularly in Sikkim in India, and in eastern Nepal. It is similar to other magpies in the genus Pica, with pied plumage with bluish-green iridescent wings and tail, but differs from the other species in the genus except for Asir magpie in having no white on the rump. It was described as a species in 1840, but had long been treated as a subspecies of Eurasian magpie, before being restored to species rank in 2018. In breeding behaviour it is largely similar to other Pica magpies, laying a single brood from April to June.


Taxonomy
The black-rumped magpie was first described by Adolphe Delessert in 1840 as a species Pica bottanensis. In 1877, Richard Bowdler Sharpe dismissed it as a synonym of the Eurasian magpie, but this was strongly disputed by Allan Octavian Hume in 1877 and 1880, maintaining it as a distinct species. It remained accepted as a distinct species (with also probably the first citation of the English name 'Black-rumped Magpie') in Eugene W. Oates's 1889 The fauna of British India, including Ceylon and Burma. By 1928, Frank Ludlow cited it as a subspecies of Eurasian magpie, Pica pica bottanensis, as did E. C. Stuart Baker in his Nidification of Birds of the Indian Empire in 1932; that then remained the accepted status until a molecular phylogenetic study published in 2018 restored it to species rank, finding that it was a sister taxon to the Asir magpie from southwestern Saudi Arabia. It was later accepted as a full species by the IOC World Bird List in version 8.2. Its genus, Pica, comes from the Latin word pica, meaning "magpie". Its species name bottanensis is from the then-used French name for Bhutan, Bottan or Boutan.


Description
The black-rumped magpie is black-and-white, with its namesake black rump, brighter plumage, stouter bill, and shorter tail being the main distinctive features from the closely related Oriental and Eurasian magpies. Its wings and tail are black with greeish-blue to purple iridescence, and large white stripes on the primary feathers visible in flight, while the flanks and central underparts are white. The wings are around 233–250 mm (9.2–9.8 in) for females and 241–259 mm (9.5–10.2 in) for males, with tails being 250 mm (9.8 in).


Vocal behaviour
Similar to many other corvids, the black-rumped magpie has a subdued, warbling song that is sometimes interrupted with high-pitched notes and rarely mimicry. Mainly sung by unpaired individuals, these songs are only audible at close range. Its typical call is a "raucous, explosive rapid chatter". 


Distribution and habitat

The black-rumped magpie is found at high altitudes, with its entire range in or on the margins of the Tibetan Plateau, from central Bhutan to Qinghai and western Sichuan in west-central China, to eastern Tibet. It has been reported from Sikkim, with three specimens reportedly collected there by Louis Mandelli in 1873, but their exact collection location is unknown and may have been in Tibet. One in Kalimpong district, West Bengal, on 9 May 2023 was the first verified Indian record.
It is found in farmland and other semi-open habitat like farming districts, parks or gardens, and usually close to human habitation, at least in Bhutan. They are uncommon in the highest altitudes, but have been observed as high as 4,800 m (15,700 ft), with nests at 4,400 m (14,400 ft).


Behaviour
Black-rumped magpie is monogamous, forming long-term pairs that persist across nesting seasons and even flocking. Like other magpies, they are social birds, often ecountered in pairs or family parties, and sometimes communal roosts of up to 30 birds.
Nest construction, handled by both sexes, begins in late March, with a single brood of eggs being laid around April to June. Clutches are usually 4–5 eggs; however, there can be as many or as few as 6 and 3 eggs. Black-rumped magpie nests are massive, domed structures, composed of twigs and plant fibres. Nests will occasionally be built on top of older nests.
The diet is largely carnivorous, consisting mainly of invertebrates, lizards, small mammals, frogs, other birds, and carrion. They primarily feed on the ground, side-hopping to catch prey, and holding their tail upwards while hunting. They occasionally perch on cattle and sheep to feed on ectoparasites.


Conservation
The black-rumped magpie remains considered a subspecies of the Eurasian magpie by BirdLife International, which classifies it with that as least concern.


References

[[File:Black-rumped Magpie (Pica bottanensis), Kanda Shan, Qinghai, China 4.jpg|thumb|In flight, showing the lack of any white on the rump; Qinghai, China]]
The '''black-rumped magpie''' ('''''Pica bottanensis''''') is a species of [[magpie]] native to west-central China and Bhutan, and possibly also irregularly in Sikkim in India, and in eastern Nepal. It is similar to other magpies in the genus ''[[Pica (bird)|Pica]]'', with pied plumage with bluish-green iridescent wings and tail, but differs from the other species in the genus except for [[Asir magpie]] in having no white on the rump. It was described as a species in 1840, but had long been treated as a [[subspecies]] of [[Eurasian magpie]], before being restored to species rank in 2018. In breeding behaviour it is largely similar to other ''Pica'' magpies, laying a single brood from April to June.

== Taxonomy ==
The black-rumped magpie was first described by [[Adolphe Delessert]] in 1840 as a species ''Pica bottanensis''.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Société Cuvierienne. |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/13714104 |title=Revue zoologique |last2=Cuvierienne |first2=Société |date=1838 |publisher=Société cuvierienne |location=Paris}}</ref> In 1877, [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe]] dismissed it as a [[synonym (taxonomy)|synonym]] of the [[Eurasian magpie]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sharpe |first1=Richard Bowdler |title=15. Pica. |journal=Catalogue of the Birds in the British Museum |date=1877 |volume=3 |page=62 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8305460 |access-date=18 February 2026}}</ref> but this was strongly disputed by [[Allan Octavian Hume]] in 1877 and 1880, maintaining it as a distinct species.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hume |first1=Allan Octavian |title=Catalogue of the Birds in the British Museum, By R. Bowdler Sharpe.  Vol. III. (review) |journal=Stray Feathers |date=1877 |volume=5 |pages=281–283 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30121459 |access-date=18 February 2026}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hume |first1=Allan Octavian |title=Notes. [Note on ''Pica bottanensis'', Deles.] |journal=Stray Feathers |date=1880 |volume=9 |pages=293–295 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/29834628 |access-date=18 February 2026}}</ref> It remained accepted as a distinct species (with also probably the first citation of the English name 'Black-rumped Magpie'<ref name="PrysJones"/>) in [[Eugene W. Oates]]'s 1889 ''The fauna of British India, including Ceylon and Burma''.<ref name="Oates">{{cite book |last1=Oates |first1=Eugene William |title=The fauna of British India, including Ceylon and Burma. Birds. |volume=1 |publisher=Taylor and Francis |publication-place=London |year=1889 |doi=10.5962/bhl.title.8366 |page=[https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/20628609 25] }}</ref> By 1928, [[Frank Ludlow]] cited it as a [[subspecies]] of Eurasian magpie, ''Pica pica bottanensis'',<ref name="Ludlow">{{Cite journal |last=Ludlow |first=Frank |date=1928 |title=V.—Birds of the Gyantse Neighbourhood, Southern Tibet.—Part II |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1474-919X.1928.tb08709.x |journal=Ibis |language=en |volume=70 |issue=1 |pages=51–73 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1928.tb08709.x |issn=1474-919X|url-access=subscription }}</ref> as did [[E. C. Stuart Baker]] in his ''Nidification of Birds of the Indian Empire'' in 1932;<ref name="Baker">{{cite web |title=Nidification of Birds of the Indian Empire 1 : E. C. Stuart Baker |website=Internet Archive |date=1932 |url=https://archive.org/details/NidificationOfBirdsOfTheIndianEmpire1/page/n49/mode/2up |access-date=2026-02-18 |page=24}}</ref> that then remained the accepted status until a [[molecular phylogenetic]] study published in 2018 restored it to species rank, finding that it was a [[sister taxon]] to the [[Asir magpie]] from southwestern Saudi Arabia.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Song | first1=S. | last2=Zhang | first2=R. | last3=Alström | first3=P. | last4=Irestedt | first4=M. | last5=Cai | first5=T. | last6=Qu | first6=Y. | last7=Ericson | first7=P.G.P. | last8=Fjeldså | first8=J. | last9=Lei | first9=F. | year=2017 | title=Complete taxon sampling of the avian genus ''Pica'' (magpies) reveals ancient relictual populations and synchronous Late-Pleistocene demographic expansion across the Northern Hemisphere | journal=Journal of Avian Biology | volume=49 | issue=2 | pages=1–14 | doi=10.1111/jav.01612 | doi-access=free }}</ref> It was later accepted as a full species by the [[IOC World Bird List]] in version 8.2.<ref name="IOC">{{Cite web |title=Jayshrike, shrikes, crows, mudnesters, melampittas, Ifrit, birds-of-paradise – IOC World Bird List |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/crows/ |access-date=2026-01-21 |website=www.worldbirdnames.org}}</ref> Its genus, ''Pica'', comes from the Latin word {{Lang|la|pica}}, meaning "magpie". Its species name ''bottanensis'' is from the then-used [[French language|French]] name for [[Bhutan]], {{Lang|fr|Bottan}} or {{Lang|fr|Boutan}}.<ref name="BoW">{{Cite journal |last=Kirwan |first=Guy M. |last2=Madge |first2=Steve |last3=Christie |first3=David |date=2025 |title=Black-rumped Magpie (''Pica bottanensis''), version 1.1 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/eurmag6/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.eurmag6.01.1 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Description ==
The black-rumped magpie is black-and-white, with its namesake black rump, brighter plumage, stouter bill, and shorter tail being the main distinctive features from the closely related [[Oriental magpie|Oriental]] and [[Eurasian magpie|Eurasian]] magpies.<ref name="BoW"/> Its [[wing]]s and tail are black with greeish-blue to purple iridescence, and large white stripes on the primary feathers visible in flight, while the flanks and central underparts are white.<ref name="BoW" /> The wings are around {{Convert|233-250|mm|in|abbr=on}} for females and {{Convert|241-259|mm|in|abbr=on}} for males, with tails being {{Convert|250|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Schäfer |first1=Ernst |last2=de Schauensee |first2=Rodolphe Meyer |date=1938 |title=Zoological Results of the Second Dolan Expedition to Western China and Eastern Tibet, 1934-1936. Part II: Birds |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/4064251 |journal=Proceedings of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia |volume=90 |pages=185–260 |issn=0097-3157}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=Vaurie |first1=Charles |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/bibliography/209901 |title=Systematic notes on Palearctic birds |last2=Vaurie |first2=Charles |date=1955 |publisher=American Museum of Natural History |location=New York, N.Y.}}</ref>

=== Vocal behaviour ===
Similar to many other corvids, the black-rumped magpie has a subdued, warbling song that is sometimes interrupted with high-pitched notes and rarely mimicry. Mainly sung by unpaired individuals, these songs are only audible at close range.<ref name="Ludlow"/> Its typical call is a "raucous, explosive rapid chatter".<ref name="BoW" /> 

== Distribution and habitat ==
[[File:Black-rumped Magpie (Pica bottanensis), Bumthang, Bhutan 04.jpg|left|thumb|Nest building in Bumthang, Bhutan]]
The black-rumped magpie is found at high altitudes, with its entire range in or on the margins of the [[Tibetan Plateau]], from central [[Bhutan]] to [[Qinghai]] and western [[Sichuan]] in west-central [[China]], to eastern [[Tibet Autonomous Region|Tibet]].<ref name="ioc">{{cite web |year=2018 |editor1-last=Gill |editor1-first=Frank |editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) |editor2-last=Donsker |editor2-first=David |title=Crows, mudnesters, birds-of-paradise |url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/crows/ |access-date=30 June 2018 |work=World Bird List Version 8.1 |publisher=International Ornithologists' Union}}</ref><ref name="BoW" /> It has been reported from [[Sikkim]], with three specimens reportedly collected there by Louis Mandelli in 1873, but their exact collection location is unknown and may have been in Tibet.<ref name="PrysJones">{{cite journal |last1=Prŷs-Jones |first1=Robert P. |last2=Rasmussen |first2=Pamela C. |last3=Rahut |first3=Biswapriya |journal=Indian Birds |volume=14 |pages=71–73 |title=The status of the Black-rumped Magpie ''Pica'' (''pica'') ''bottanensis'' in India |url=https://indianbirds.in/pdfs/IB_14_3_PrysJones_Rasmussen_BlackrumpedMagpie.pdf |access-date=2026-02-18}}</ref> One in [[Kalimpong district]], West Bengal, on 9 May 2023 was the first verified Indian record.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Bhattacharyya |first1=Pramit Kumar |journal=Indian Birds |volume=19 |page=49 |title=The Black-rumped Magpie ''Pica bottanensis'' in Kalimpong District, West Bengal: An addition to the Indian avifauna |url=https://indianbirds.in/pdfs/IB_19_2_Bhattacharyya_BlackrumpedMagpie.pdf |access-date=2026-02-18}}</ref>

It is found in farmland and other semi-open habitat like farming districts, parks or gardens, and usually close to human habitation, at least in Bhutan.<ref name="PrysJones"/> They are uncommon in the highest altitudes, but have been observed as high as {{Convert|4800|m|ft|abbr=on}}, with nests at {{Convert|4400|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name="BoW" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Lu |first1=Xin |last2=Ke |first2=Dianhua |last3=Ma |first3=Xiaoyan |last4=Gong |first4=Guohong |last5=Yu |first5=Tonglei |date=2023-04-24 |title=Nesting records of 20 bird species in Lhasa region, Tibet |url=http://www.j-avianres.com/en/article/doi/10.5122/cbirds.2010.0011.pdf |journal=Avian Research |language=en |volume=1 |issue=3 |pages=167–174 |doi=10.5122/cbirds.2010.0011 |issn=2055-6187}}</ref>

== Behaviour ==
Black-rumped magpie is [[monogamous]], forming long-term pairs that persist across nesting seasons and even flocking.<ref name="BoW" /> Like other magpies, they are social birds, often ecountered in pairs or family parties, and sometimes communal roosts of up to 30 birds.<ref name="Ludlow" /><ref name="BoW" />

Nest construction, handled by both sexes, begins in late March, with a single brood of eggs being laid around April to June. Clutches are usually 4–5 eggs; however, there can be as many or as few as 6 and 3 eggs.<ref name="Ludlow"/><ref name="BoW" /> Black-rumped magpie nests are massive, domed structures, composed of twigs and plant fibres. Nests will occasionally be built on top of older nests.<ref name="BoW" /><ref name="Ludlow" />

The diet is largely [[carnivorous]], consisting mainly of [[Invertebrate|invertebrates]], lizards, small mammals, frogs, other birds, and carrion. They primarily feed on the ground, side-hopping to catch prey, and holding their tail upwards while hunting. They occasionally perch on cattle and sheep to feed on ectoparasites.<ref name="BoW" />

== Conservation ==
The black-rumped magpie remains considered a subspecies of the [[Eurasian magpie]] by [[BirdLife International]], which classifies it with that as [[Least-concern species|least concern]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Eurasian Magpie Pica Pica Species Factsheet |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/eurasian-magpie-pica-pica |access-date=2026-01-21 |website=BirdLife DataZone |language=en}}</ref>

zh.extract:
青藏喜鹊（Black-rumped magpie，Pica bottanensis）是分布于不丹中部至中华人民共和国西部的一种喜鹊属動物。它以前一度被認為是喜鹊的一个亚种。2018年发表的一项分子系统发生学研究发现，青藏喜鹊是分佈於沙特阿拉伯西南部的阿拉伯喜鹊的姐妹群。
